facts.text: New Hackensack’s heart sits at the crossroads of All Angels Hill Road, New Hackensack Road, and Route 376, a spot officially called New Hackensack Square.
The New Hackensack Reformed Church, founded in 1758, is one of only three original Dutch churches that still stand in Dutchess County.
In 1775, Jacob Griffin’s Tavern in Swartwoutville hosted “Rendezvous” meetings where Tories and the Committee of Safety plotted a spy network.
The hamlet’s first railroad station opened there, marking the beginning of regional rail travel for New Hackensack residents.
South Mountain Road, a winding two‑lane historic road that runs through New Hackensack, earned a spot on the National Register of Historic Places.
The Tea House, still standing, showcases carvings of mountains, windmills, and 1930s icons like Popeye, echoing the area’s Dutch‑American heritage.
